SAP Managed Tags:

entry.title: The Procurement Monthly - June 2024
entry.content:
Summary

The reports were good again this month!

Great range this month, from the importance (and challenges of AI) to the evolution of category management and the future direction of external workforce.

What is interesting is how well many of these reports tie into the economist research covered last month.  

Gartner CPO E Book

This key issue guide from Gartner looks at 3 top issues impacting CPO and their teams. Namely

Gen AI – Where and when to prioritise investments.

Change Fatigue – Balance aspirations with frameworks to overcome fatigue.

Procurement Transformation – Faster than ever.

The prism that is used to describe the value v feasibility of the Gen AI opportunities is excellent, though id argue the feasibility is linked to maturity of the function and the tech stack,

I really like the visual showing how much more complex the procurement value proposition has become and linking this to the change framework is a useful idea.

And a nice stat to finish!

Chief Procurement Officers who design simplicity into procurement transformations enjoy a 42% increase in transformation success over those who don’t.

GEP AI Guide for procurement transformation

Continuing the AI theme this research covers insights into the importance of AI for competitive advantage.

After recently attending the world procurement congress many conversations centred around this topic, and how to prioritise, what are the benefits where are the constraints what are the positive and negative impacts, and where could it lead?  

The research that in terms of adoption, the landscape is diverse across multiple stages of adoption from exploration, pilots and implementations, I think showcasing the newness and polarising views that many have.

Barriers have been written about previously, notably in a world commerce and contracting paper earlier this year however its interesting to note the consistency around data management, technology itself and cultural implications. 

“Culture eats strategy for breakfast”.

Cultural resistance within organizations is a notable challenge, identified by 36% of professionals. Overcoming apprehension or reluctance towards AI adoption is essential for the successful integration of AI into procurement processes.

Much has also been written about automation and augmentation. This shows up again here with 42% identifying automation assistance as a key outcome, and the same number identifying augmentation (enhancement) as an outcome.

As identified In the barriers section data was a big barrier and the report has a readiness section on infrastructure, that again shows a very diffused readiness landscape. 26% working with basic digital systems that need an overhaul and 21% are starting at the beginning. Perhaps this is a key driver of the recent Economist Impact report that identified that Digitalisation was a top priority for procurement in the  next 12-18 months.

Another big takeaway both from this report and also the events recently has been the AI Skill sets, and this is covered in this report identifying that 31% have some familiarity with AI and 19% have none. Skills and how to develop them, will be at the forefront of many procurement leaders’ agendas, as it has been for some time.

Kearny COO Report

This study is 2nd study done by Kearny into the COO’s. Reason I’m including is that it does give some valuable insights into organisations that procurement leaders will need to consider their alignment, and as pointed out by the recent Economist impact research, CPO now report into COO more than previously!

Some key takeaways:

Over 90% of respondents are prioritising ew customers and new channels to drive growth

51% of COO are targeting 5-7% cost savings in 2024 (and 22% targeting 8-9%)!

Cost still wins v risk but the gap is closing across 4 areas (Inventory, single sourcing, nearshoring, transportation)

70% (an increase over previous year) identified forming alliances with key existing suppliers as the top way to mitigation inflation.

 AQPC Spend Analysis

AQPC have produced 2 pieces around spend analysis. Spend analysis was identified by the Economist as the top driver of digitalisation in procurement for the last 2 years so its worthwhile taking a look at this.

The current state document identifies that 43% of respondents indicated their classification is semi automate and 48% automated, and 47% report that their data cleanings is semi aut0omated and the same number fully automated. As per the GEP report earlier, 60% if respondents identified a significant or very significant impact of data quality on their outcomes, confirming the core essentiality of spend data.

The what to do about it article identifies steps to improve spend data quality, from standardisation of coding systems, to full automation of spend analysis and cleanliness

AQPC AP Benchmarks

The cross industry AP key benchmarks report was also released by AQPC this month and is a great resource to benchmark against. I have compared these v the SAP Ariba Benchmarks and (depending on your view on DPO) shows the improved performance that SAP can deliver v these benchmarks.

 

 

SIA Workforce Buyer Survey

This is an annual study, and ive compared this with last year as its interesting to not some of the changes.

In terms of who owns contingent labour programs, it has moved a little more toward being HR focused, compared to 2023 with 49% stating its HR compared with 46% last year. Procurement was selected 37%.

You may remember from last year we asked the same question in the Economist research for large enterprises it was 42/40 for HR and procurement. Overall (including mid-sized) it was 46/30, and whilst the SIA report doesn’t give org revenue sizes it does give contingent labour sizes and it is more skewed toward LE than mid-sized

Looking at the size of contingent labour again I show the comparison with last year which shows that if you look just at the SIA numbers, they are meeting what they said the previous year with 21% today, 22% in 2 years from 2023 and in 2024 today is at then 22% indicated to be hit in 2 years from last year.

Both have around 26/27% in 10 years’ time. These % represent the % of workforce that is contingent.

When we look at the Economist data, we see it as a % of org spend, and it shows that its between 5-15% of total org spend (including employee spend) and that is shared by 75% of 2300 respondents. To put that in perspective if an org spend is $1billion, then they will spend between 50 million and 150 million on their contingent workforce spend and according to SIA that number is growing every year.

So why is it growing?! What are the priorities for contingent workforce –

According to the report, reducing and controlling costs is the top answer and then implementing technology to manage (and deliver) those cost savings.

Within the Economist research we asked a similar question (with differing options) and the top answer from a contingent perspective was balancing internal and external workers and direct sourcing.

You can see the balancing twice in the priorities from the SIA report – Integrating into corporate planning and total talent management.

SIA reports on direct sourcing separately, and interesting in the Economist direct sourcing was identified by 62% of respondents as the top priority in the next 3-5 years (and 59% of CHRO) and you can see the expected growth in that area from 12% today, to 27% in 10 years’ time, suggesting that direct sourcing and talent networks will be more heavily relied upon in the future.

Category Management – Future Purchasing

This report is a biannual report into Category management which as you will know has consistently appeared in the Economist  surveys as both a driver of digitation (4th this year) and as a top tech trend to be implemented in next 12 -18 months (top last year 4th this year).

The report is detailed, and has some interesting insights:

The trend for leaders and starters has been a reduction in their category management capability index scores (by 9% for leaders and 5% for improvers). This may be due to the sample of respondents, additional questions we have asked, or a slight removal of focus on category management while dealing with operational challenges, but there is an overall trend downwards of some outcomes.

  • This year 86% said stakeholders didn’t know their role, 2 years ago it was 80%
  • Ave savings rate fallen from 7.4 to 7.1%. But this is likely due to inflation.
  • Ave spend per cat man is 101M Euro up from 73m Euro.
  • Interesting that technical category management skills is the number one area to improve 2 years ago it was behavioural skills.
  • 68% of value remains untapped.

The team at future purchasing have segmented their 300+ responses into an order based on outcomes generated and essentially this is the starters (majority of respondents at 74%), improvers the next 19% and leaders which are the top 7%

Early in the report is a summary of some key metrics:

Spend per supplier: Category management is often thought of as a mechanism to increase leverage through consolidation. It is notable that the spend per supplier for leaders of €1.06m is 5x that of starters and 3x that of improvers. This concentration of spend not only increases leverage but also allows increased intensity of SRM improvement activity with key suppliers — an additional source of value that leaders can access.

Spend per procurement team head: The efficiency of the leaders is demonstrated by the fact that €76m spend is managed per member of the procurement team, versus €34m for starters and €27m for improvers. This equates to 55% and 64% fewer resources for the spend managed — reducing the cost to procure. The drop-off in efficiency for improvers versus starters is consistent with previous surveys and may be due to the intensity which they are pursuing value delivery, i.e., effectiveness versus procurement team efficiency.

% of staff focused on category management: Leaders have a higher percentage of staff focused on category management than starters or improvers (43% vs 32% and 36%). Hence, they have more time to spend creating and actioning high quality category strategies, engaging stakeholders, and strategically managing suppliers.

Spend per category manager: Leaders’ category managers are responsible for €177m spend versus €106m for starters and €75m for improvers. This equates to 40% and 58% fewer category managers for the spend managed and reinforces the resource efficiency of leaders.

Looking at overall capability

HIGHEST SCORING CAPABILITY AREA

Across all maturity groups, CatMan strategy is the highest-scoring dimension, with an average of 3.2. This may be a result of Procurement being able to control most of the levers in this area.

LOWEST SCORING CAPABILITY AREA

Stakeholder engagement is the dimension with the joint lowest average score, at 2.7. Practices in this dimension are critical for procurement teams to influence, such as stakeholders having sufficient time for category management, making it a core business process for stakeholders, and category strategies being co-created with business stakeholders.

The joint lowest score is process & technology where even leaders show a considerable variation in the way that category management is deployed. Building a strong process and ensuring consistency of application through good governance and review can help significantly with quality, stakeholder communications, and adoption.

Interestingly process management was the 2nd highest response in improvements needed in category management for CPOs in the latest Economist research.

Ive pulled out three interesting stats from the tech section of the report:

86% of improvers (and 100% of leaders) include process change and supplier management, as well as sourcing in their implementation options versus 47% in our process, category of starters, showing that as organisations become more mature their strategic options expand away from just sourcing.

34% of improvers (and 50% of leaders) use a digitalised category management process & toolkit versus 8% of starters.

14% of improvers (and 20% of leaders) have a category management process and toolkit that uses artificial intelligence to recommend value levers versus 5% our category management of starters.

 

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

entry.title: SAP Ariba: How to import Analytical Reports from Test to Production realms
entry.content:
Hi Everyone,

Often during SAP Ariba implementation, we create several Analytical Reports in Ariba Test realm as per customer's requirements. However, it becomes a redundant task to create those same reports in Production realm during cutover.

This blog explains the steps to import Analytical Reports from Ariba test realm to Production realm.

Pre-requisite:

Parameter Application.Analysis.ReportDumpFromUIEnabled must be set as Yes in Ariba Test and Production realms.

This parameter is controlled by SAP Ariba Support team and you need to create a case to set this parameter value as Yes.

Procedure:

Step 1: In your Test realm, move all the reports which you want to export in a new folder under ‘Public Reports’ folder

Step 2: Click on the folder 🡪 Click ‘Action’ under Create select Export Reports in Folder to File and select a destination to save in your local system and save the file.

Step 3: In your Production realm, under Public Reports create a folder in which you want to import the reports.

Step 4: Click on the folder 🡪 Click Action > under Create select Import Reports from File to Folder 🡪 Select the file you saved in your local system and then click Import

Following additional points needs to be ensured before importing the reports from one realm to another:

  • Ensure you have Full System Groups assigned for Report Administration. 
  • Ensure Reports Exported does not contain any Custom Fields, Custom Measures, and Custom Measure Calculations.
  • Avoid Reports with Fact Sources not available in Destination Realm.
  • Ensure Reports Exported does not contain Filter Criteria for selected values only existing in Source Realm
  • Avoid using Personal Workspace due to potential conflicts or limitations of User Roles, Permissions, or Folder Access.
  • Ensure Report UNIQUE Owner ID exist in both Source + Destination Realm. To ensure uniqueness, it is safe to have all Report Owner as functional Owner Username (Customer Support Admin). Owner of a Report Name can be changed under Attributes for a Report Name. Change to Owner (Customer Support Admin) requires this User to perform this.
  • Ensure the content in the Folder Exported ONLY contain plain SAP Ariba Reports (i.e. not other Document Formats).

 

SAP Ariba Sourcing SAP Ariba Spend Analysis SAP Ariba Procurement

entry.title: The Procurement Monthly - July 2024
entry.content:
Summary

An interesting set of reports this month. There is a focus on AP with some themes emerging around the need to automate especially around invoice approvals and time to pay. Exception management is still a big issue.

There is a focus on fraud in a number of reports, with some identifying the rising cases of fraud, potentially driven by AI.

Several reports identify budgets for procurement technology are either increasing or staying the same.

Automation and digitisation are common themes across all reports, and as per last month where there comparable benchmarks, ive pulled them all together and compared then with SAP benchmarks from our program.

IDC SAAS Path

This paper provides visibility into the mind and journey of the Software as a Service (SaaS) buyer across multiple functional applications. For my summary ill be focussing on the procurement aspect.

According to the report, Procurement application buyers are willing to spend more for advanced AI capabilities with strong interest in applications that drive cost savings and improve operation efficiency. They seek quick time to value from trusted brands that offer robust data security and ease of implementation. They have shown an increased appetite to explore the functionality of Spend Orchestration applications that can improve the user experience and provide great visibility to all spend.

There are other personas also listed, including CLM, Travel and expense.

Key Barriers for adoption include industry specificity, price, cloud preferences and data security. Following up on the Economist study which identified that 71% stated that procurement was involved in the digital transformation of the organisation, this report identifies the roles (IT and LOB) roles and confirms the involvement of procurement, in evaluation, recommendation and final decision all being over 34%.

The paper also confirms that the majority (51%) planned to increase spend on procurement applications with 43% planning to keep spending where it currently was. Key attributes that buyers looked for in procurement included trusted Brand & robust data security  (29%), Value for money (27%) and ease of integration (25%).

Overall an interesting report that taps into buyers preferences across a range of LoB.

Fraud & Payments Studies

There has been a few reports this month about fraud and payments and the next couple of sections will cover them.

The first is from a PYMNTS study that identified that nearly 60% of large firms use at least five systems for AP. It also identified that executing payments was the step in the source to pay cycle that CFO (23.3%) experienced most delays in last year followed by authorising payments (18.3%).

Manual AP handling is still the norm. Just 17% of enterprise firms run their source-to-pay systems largely without human involvement.

Ravelin also launched their fraud and payments survey for 2024 and also had some interesting points, with fraud rising for 75% of companies and payment fraud up by 69%. AI powered fraud affected 64% of respondents  but 66% found ML useful in fraud prevention. The majority of sectors identified that impacts on revenue and loss of profit was the top pain point with 30.3% identifying costs of between $5-10 million pa and 27% stat8ing it was over $15m.

These two reports are good intros into the rest of the AP reports this month.

AQPC Late Payments

In APQC research, the number of finance and accounting managers who pointed to late customer payments as a priority issue in managing cashflow increased by 10 percentage points between 2022 and 2023. Potential causes for the heightened level of concern over late customer payments include:

  • Economic factors, including recession fears, high interest rates, and decreased liquidity.
  • Supply chain disruptions, which can result in the need to increase inventory or delay work, among other implications.
  • Changes in payment culture and the broad shift toward longer payment terms.

The report identifies some suggestions for managing this, including providing incentives for early payment/prompt payment.

Interesting 55% of finance leaders identified cash flow management as a top priority for finance to focus in for 2024 with 60% identifying process improvement.

Ardent Partners State of AP/Metrics that Matter

Two reports from Ardent Partners the State of Accounts payable and AP Metrics that Matter.

Both reports are interesting but for the purposes of this summary, I wont distinguish between them as they do cover some of the same information (albeit from differing number of respondents).

Top priorities for AP in 2024, Automation (43%), which is similar to the AQPC report above and also talks to procurement report from the Economist where it also identified digitisation as the top short term priority.

Improving data and reporting is second( 41%) with enabling suppliers more readily. Notably for me, was the 5th priority being improve collaboration and communication with procurement (31%)  which  I think is very interesting especially as the shared services operating model is not currently the one that organisations are moving toward for procurement and AP meaning that this is absolutely essential to ensure that automation, process improvements and digitisation are done in consultation so as not to negatively impact each other.

Top barriers for AP include the high% of exceptions (53%), and the approvals taking too long (41%) which is a call back to the PYMNTS report earlier. Continuing the fraud theme this is 3rd at 29%.

State of Procurement Function

This e book from Jaggaer follows a survey of 220 leaders to understand the strategic role of procurement. Similar to the IDC report earlier 75% of organisations worldwide have increased or maintained their budgets for procurement technology according to the paper.

Other interesting points include that 42% stated that highly manual processes is the number 1 challenge holding procurement back which is a contributing factor I think when looking at the Economist report earlier this year that 57% identified digitalisation as the top short term priority.

The 2nd challenge holding procurement back is a tie between, cumbersome and slow comms with suppliers, a lack of information and data flow between systems (integration) and inaccurate supplier data. All of these tied at 37%.

This also has call outs, to a previous IDC report on the power of networks to improve communications with suppliers as well as data quality, and the IDC report above on ease of integration being a key need form buyers of procurement solutions.

The report goes onto identify that Spend analysis is the area most ripe for digitisation and automation, which echoes the Economist report for the last couple of years which has also identified this and as a continuation of the theme of automating invoice approvals, 31% identified that this was an area ripe for automation.

TPRM Risk Management Study

This study from Prevalent is a really interesting read and covers trends, challenges, and initiatives impacting third-party risk management (TPRM). Top takeaways include:

  • 61% of organizations reported experiencing a third-party data breach or security incident in the past year, marking a 49% increase from the previous year.
  • Many organizations struggle with inadequate resources, with only one-third of vendor relationships being managed in a TPRM program.
  • Half of the surveyed companies still rely on spreadsheets and multiple disparate tools to assess and manage their third-party relationships.
  • Despite tracking risks across the vendor lifecycle, few companies actually do anything about what they find.
  • A top concern about use of third party was data breaches or security incidents with 74% identifying this as top  issue.
  • Cybersecurity is the only risk being tracked more this year than last year (58% tracking more, 35% tracking less).

For any professionals involved in TPRM programs it’s a must read

Ardent Partners State of procurement digitization

This report, commissioned by Ivalua, looks at the state if source to pay digitisation. Savings are the top priority for the respondents with 74% identifying this as a top priority, whilst the top strategy for improving performance is to improve internal comms and collaboration, which was also identified in last year’s economist study (this years research shows that the collaboration has improved, while still room for improvement).

Similar to the Economist study, ESG programs are planned changes for next 2- 3 years (64%) with the next 4 areas all concentrating on supplier management capabilities. From risk, to relationships and intelligence.

Following the IDC and Jaggaer reports earlier, 79% state that budgets are either increasing or remaining the same.

Usability, and user experience are a top criteria for solution selection according to the report, and similar to the IDC report earlier, price (Value for money) and pricing models feature proximately and top benefits of S2P being identified as greater efficiency, (57%), improved productivity (49% leading the way. 

Benchmark Summary

I’ve gone through the reports this month and pulled together some quick benchmarks comparison between best in class and what the SAP benchmarking has as best in class through using SAP solutions.

 

Benchmark

3rd party benchmark

SAP Benchmark

Time to process invoice

3 days

.4 day

Invoice Exception Rate

9%

7.5%

Invoice processed straight through

49.2%

65.1%

Suppliers that submit invoices electronically

59%

81%

Spend under Management

68.9%

84%

Savings achieved

8.6%

9.43%

Contract compliance

67%

95%

 

The 3rd party benchmarks were collected from the reports reviewed this month and compared against comparable metrics from the SAP benchmarking service

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

entry.title: Cognitive Procurement: The Future of Smart Buying
entry.content:
Introduction

Cognitive procurement is the application of artificial intelligence (AI) and data analytics to the procurement process, enabling organizations to make smarter, faster, and more strategic decisions. Cognitive procurement can help buyers optimize their sourcing, contracting, supplier management, and risk management activities, as well as enhance their collaboration and innovation with internal and external stakeholders.

Description

Cognitive procurement leverages various technologies, such as natural language processing, machine learning, computer vision, and blockchain, to automate and augment the procurement tasks. For example, cognitive procurement can use natural language processing to analyze large volumes of unstructured data, such as contracts, invoices, emails, and social media posts, and extract relevant insights and recommendations. Machine learning can help cognitive procurement learn from past and current data, and predict future trends and outcomes. Computer vision can enable cognitive procurement to recognize and classify images, such as products, logos, or invoices, and perform quality checks or fraud detection. Blockchain can provide cognitive procurement with a secure and transparent platform for tracking and verifying transactions and contracts.

Benefits

 

Cognitive procurement can offer many benefits to organizations, such as:

  • Reducing costs and improving efficiency by automating repetitive and low-value tasks, such as data entry, invoice processing, or contract compliance.
  • Increasing savings and value by optimizing sourcing and negotiation strategies, identifying new opportunities and suppliers, and leveraging dynamic pricing and discounts.
  • Enhancing risk management and compliance by monitoring and mitigating supplier risks, ensuring contract adherence, and complying with regulatory and ethical standards.
  • Improving collaboration and innovation by facilitating communication and knowledge sharing among buyers, suppliers, and other stakeholders, and fostering a culture of continuous improvement and learning.

Challenges

However, cognitive procurement also faces some challenges, such as:

  • Data quality and availability: Cognitive procurement relies on large and diverse data sources, which may not be accurate, complete, or consistent. Therefore, cognitive procurement needs to ensure data quality and availability, and address any data gaps or errors.
  • Change management and adoption: Cognitive procurement requires a significant change in the procurement mindset and culture, as well as the skills and competencies of the procurement professionals. Therefore, cognitive procurement needs to provide adequate training and support, and address any resistance or skepticism from the users.
  • Trust and transparency: Cognitive procurement involves complex and sometimes opaque algorithms, which may not be easily understood or explained by the users. Therefore, cognitive procurement needs to ensure trust and transparency, and provide clear and meaningful explanations and feedback to the users.

Conclusion

Cognitive procurement is the future of smart buying, as it can help organizations transform their procurement process and achieve competitive advantage. However, cognitive procurement also requires careful planning and implementation, and overcoming some technical and organizational challenges.

entry.title: 2408 release for SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network Early Release Series
entry.content:
Sign up now for the 2408 Early Release Series

Follow this blog to receive notification of new series as soon as registration opens.

SAP strives to equip our customers and partners with valuable release readiness resources for each SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network quarterly release.

Early Release Series

The SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network Early Release series of webcasts cover features planned for the upcoming quarterly release.  This platform, available to customers and partners, has a clear mission: deliver an interactive forum to engage with SAP product experts providing simple, direct answers to your most pressing questions.  There is a different session for each product area with content tailored for the specific audience:

  • Supplier Management & Risk
  • Procure-to-Pay
  • Source-to-Contract
  • Business Network

If you want to learn more and actively engage with SAP subject matter experts, we encourage you to participate in these informative sessions.  Register now!

Quarterly Release Communications

The release calendar for SAP Business Network  and release calendar SAP Ariba Applications identify release timing several quarters in advance.

Notice of upcoming quarterly releases will be communicated to Buyer customers three weeks prior to the release date.  Suppliers receive notice two weeks prior.  Releases are communicated via email notification (look for subject line starting with: Advance Release Notice).

Designated Support Contacts (DSC) and anyone from your organization that have chosen to subscribe to product notifications will receive these announcements. 

Where to find out more details?

On demand replays of webcasts from completed quarterly releases can be consumed from Release Readiness Resources: Webcast Landing Page

entry.title: The Strategic Importance of Procurement in Enterprise Companies
entry.content:
Introduction

Imagine a thriving company suddenly losing millions because one key supplier failed to deliver critical components on time. This scenario isn't just hypothetical—it’s a harsh reality faced by many businesses. Inefficiencies in procurement can cost companies up to 20% of their annual revenue, underscoring how crucial this function is to a company's success.

Enterprise companies, with their extensive product lines, large scale, global customer bases, and intense competition, operate in highly complex markets where effective procurement processes are paramount.

This essay explores the strategic importance of procurement, delving into its role as a core component of any enterprise's operational structure.

Purpose

This essay aims to elucidate the crucial role procurement plays in large companies by examining how it integrates within organizational frameworks, impacts performance, collaborates with other departments, and utilizes technology.

Understanding these aspects will provide insight into why intelligent procurement strategies are fundamental to business success and how they can be optimized for better outcomes.

The Strategic Importance of Procurement

Procurement isn't just about buying things; it’s a vital link in the value chain connecting companies with their customers, suppliers, and other stakeholders. It ensures the smooth flow of materials, information, and money, acting as a bridge between the inner workings of the company and the outside world.

For instance, a major player in the automotive industry needs to source thousands of parts from a global network of suppliers to assemble their vehicles. By strategically aligning their procurement process, they maintain high production rates, ensure quality control, and manage costs efficiently. This alignment not only secures a steady supply chain but also enables the company to react swiftly to market changes and technological advancements, providing a competitive edge. Procurement supports essential functions like product development, marketing, sales, operations, and finance. When managed effectively, it aligns sourcing decisions with business goals, capitalizes on market opportunities, and creates competitive advantages.

The Role of Procurement in a Large Enterprise from a Strategic Perspective

From a strategic viewpoint, procurement helps the company achieve its vision, mission, and goals by:

Understanding Business Strategy:

Analyzing internal and external environments, strengths, and stakeholder expectations.

Let us use a pharmaceutical giant as an example, they invest in market research to understand global health trends and regulatory changes, allowing the procurement team to anticipate needs and align sourcing strategies proactively.

Developing Sourcing Strategy:

Defining objectives, evaluating suppliers, negotiating deals, and managing sourcing activities. The company establishes a strategic sourcing group to identify and evaluate suppliers based on criteria such as quality, cost, and ethical practices.

By forming strategic partnerships with specialized suppliers, the company secures vital resources for groundbreaking drug development.

Aligning Sourcing with Business Strategy:

Ensuring sourcing decisions support business goals and create synergies. Aligning procurement goals with a mission to deliver affordable healthcare solutions, the company negotiates long-term agreements to hedge against raw material price volatility, ensuring cost consistency without compromising quality or ethical standards.

Supporting Overall Strategy:

Achieving goals like market share growth and enhanced customer loyalty.

The pharmaceutical company sources from sustainable and ethical suppliers to meet its corporate social responsibility commitments. This approach enhances its brand reputation among consumers and investors, ensures compliance with industry regulations, and fosters customer loyalty.

Through these strategic initiatives, the procurement function significantly contributes to the company's overall strategy, ensuring a robust, efficient supply chain aligned with long-term goals. This seamless integration aids in achieving market growth, innovation, and customer satisfaction.

Organizational Placement of Procurement

Where procurement fits in a company can vary widely based on factors like centralization, integration, and responsibilities. These factors determine whether procurement decisions are centralized, decentralized, hybrid, matrix, or networked.

Consider a large technology firm navigating a complex global supply chain. Initially, it implemented a decentralized procurement function to grant autonomy to regional offices, allowing rapid decision-making and responsiveness to local market conditions.

However, inconsistencies in supplier quality and contractual terms increased costs and risks.

In response, the firm transitioned to a hybrid procurement model. While regional offices retained some autonomy, key procurement decisions, such as supplier selection and major contract negotiations, were centralized at headquarters. This shift leveraged the benefits of centralized control—such as standardized processes, bulk purchasing, and compliance—without sacrificing the agility offered by decentralization.

Here's a breakdown of different organizational placement strategies and their respective pros and cons:

Centralized Function: Offers control and efficiency but may lack flexibility and innovation.

Decentralized Function: Provides autonomy and agility but can lead to complexity and inconsistency.

Hybrid Function: Balances centralization and decentralization but may create confusion.

Matrix Function: Enhances collaboration but can cause ambiguity and tensions.

Network Function: Leverages external expertise and resources but comes with risks.

Through their hybrid model, the technology firm achieved a more cohesive and efficient procurement process, resulting in lower costs, standardized supplier quality, and better alignment with corporate goals.

Impact of Procurement on Enterprise Performance

Procurement significantly affects a company’s performance, profitability, and sustainability:

Cost Reduction: Better prices, consolidated purchases, and optimized inventory lower overall costs.

For example, by negotiating multi-year contracts with key suppliers, the manufacturer secures bulk discounts and stabilizes pricing, significantly reducing expenditure and enhancing budget predictability.

Quality Improvement: Choosing reliable suppliers and adhering to quality standards improve product and service quality. Rigorous supplier audits and quality checks result in high-quality components that enhance product performance and durability, elevating customer satisfaction and brand reputation.

Risk Mitigation: Conducting thorough checks and diversifying supply sources manage and minimize risks. By diversifying its supply base across multiple geographies, the manufacturer mitigates risks associated with geopolitical instability, natural disasters, and supplier insolvency. This approach ensures a resilient supply chain capable of withstanding disruptions.

Driving Innovation: Finding new technologies and collaborating with suppliers fosters innovation. Collaborating with cutting-edge tech firms and startups, the company integrates groundbreaking technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Internet of Things (IoT) into their products, maintaining a competitive edge in a fast-evolving market.

Creating Value: Aligning sourcing strategies with business goals creates and delivers value. By sourcing sustainable materials and engaging in ethical procurement practices, the company meets regulatory requirements and enhances its corporate social responsibility profile, appealing to eco-conscious consumers and investors.

Through strategic procurement practices, the manufacturer transforms procurement from a cost center into a dynamic element of competitive advantage, bolstering overall enterprise performance and sustainability.

Conclusion

Procurement is essential for enterprise companies, influencing strategic direction, operational efficiency, and value creation.

Its successful integration and effectiveness depend on factors like centralization and collaboration. Smart procurement can reduce costs, improve quality, mitigate risks, drive innovation, and create value.

Recall the scenario of the thriving company losing millions due to a key supplier's failure. If that company had optimized its procurement processes, it could have avoided such a catastrophic loss. By investing in advanced technologies, fostering collaboration between departments, and aligning procurement strategies with business goals, companies can navigate complex markets and achieve lasting success.

Industry leaders and stakeholders must recognize the strategic importance of procurement and take proactive steps to enhance its role within their organizations. Strengthening your procurement strategies today will secure your business’s future, preventing you from being the next example of inefficiencies.

If you wish to view this in a video format, please go to https://sapvideo.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/?entry_id=1_we7gk2nm

Lars

entry.title: Adopting Guided Sourcing
entry.content:
Dear readers,


Let me introduce myself, my name is Muhamed Sabic and I am part of the Solution Value Advisory Team for EMEA/MEE. I have recently been trained in Guided Sourcing and wanted to share my learning experience with you!


As I set off on my mission to learn about Guided Sourcing, how to set up and use the solution I started by reviewing the Guided Sourcing Transition Toolkit. Here I gathered some great insights on what’s new, the enhanced features, as well as how these changes will impact various stakeholders. The toolkit is excellent, it provided clear documentation with videos that explain to users how to enable Guided Sourcing, review templates, and activate the newly integrated AI technologies.


After having gained some background knowledge about the solution I was ready to get hands on and start testing. To help me with this I navigated to the Spend Management and Business Network Value Map where I found a neatly structured training plan. I was provided with a combination of reading material, demos and step by step guides which helped me create multiple sourcing events using various rules and event types.
I further educated myself on Guided Sourcing and the end-to-end process including creating a sourcing project, publishing events and the award procedure by reviewing the Guided Sourcing End-to-End Demo, where all the above steps are covered in detail.


If you or someone in your organization wants to learn more about Guided Sourcing, please follow the links I have shared in this article. Additional links and information can be found in the Guided Sourcing Transition Toolkit and Spend Management and Business Network Value Map. You will need to follow the below guide in order to access the Spend Management and Business Network Value Map please go here! Take advantage of the self-service content to help you develop an expert understanding of Guided Sourcing, the new features and capabilities.


Thanks for reading!
Muhamed Sabic

entry.title: Spend Trends September 2024
entry.content:
Summary

A quieter month on the report front, largely because it was summer holidays in the northern hemisphere, but what we lack in quantity, we make up for in quality!

First we start with a very hand buying guide giving insights into a range of categories that will help us understand the issues many of these buyers are facing. Before we look into the range of trends and challenges that companies and procurement face and that for many its time to reinvent their strategy as the disruption and geopolitical climate has impacted existing strategies effectiveness.

One of the main areas that will always be on the table is supplier management and performance, and whilst there are several reports that conflict, the importance is undeniable. If companies want to embark on cost out programs they will need the support of the suppliers and supply chain to be successful.

Finally what does great look like? Well digital great is outlined in the final report, and for me that’s the read of the month!

S&P Q3 Buying Strategies

This was a webinar I attended and one of the resources weas a really interesting guide into a whole variety of commodities and categories of spend and events over last few months.

The commodities include, Energy, Metals, Steels, Building materials and it gives a really good broad understanding of some of the market pressures across the market such as…

 “the two primary causes of this are the divergence of the construction spending outlook for different sectors and the size of price growth throughout the pandemic.”

Whilst this doesn’t cover all of the sub areas, it does give you considerable background into what’s happening broadly and of course you can reach out to S&P for a more detailed review.

AQPC Direct & Indirect Trends

This report provides a cross-industry snapshot of the current state of direct and indirect procurement, including procurement challenges, supplier relationship management, deployment of purchasing application, cloud environment & use of technologies in procurement processes. It surveyed 285 participants in the research, which is a great number.

The report stated that over two-fifths of respondents identified shortages or delays in raw materials and/or cybersecurity risks to be among the top three challenges in procurement currently experienced by their business entities. Complexity in data management is the third most common challenge.

Whilst there was commonalty in the focus areas for supplier relationships, they were inverted depending in direct or indirect. Quality and delivery were the top 2 in that order for directs, whereas for indirect delivery was more important. Interesting to note that price focus was 2% and 1% which is different from many other studies.

Other key takeaways included 71% of orgs tracked tail spend (im surprised its not more).

When it comes to emerging tech, most organisations are in the evaluation phase rather than piloting or implementing, again whereas for more mature tech like RPA, more are in the piloting phase for Directs, but in the considering phase for Indirect. Its interesting how few are in the operating or implementing phase.

CAPS

If you haven’t heard of CAPS they are the Centre for advanced purchasing statistics (right up my street!)

 

They produce a regular stream of research, that is available to members (im not a member sadly) but you can still get the top sheets. This month they have had a couple of top sheets that leads back to a metrics of supply management study

One is an average cost savings per sourcing employee which averages $6.9Million per employee and ranges from 6.3 million for manufacturing, and 8.8 million for services.

 

They also shared their organisational design statistics which has 49% Centre led which is similar to the Economist study showing CoE as the lead followed by Centre Led.

Procuretech – Elevating Supplier Performance

This 100 person study sought to explore the strength, or absence, of current supplier engagement strategies and how they manage their relationships with suppliers to deliver high performance.

Some interesting key takeaways

  • 37% analyse supplier performance on a monthly basis
  • 72% state that supplier performance data has unlocked improvements in supply resilience
  • 100% use at least three tools to integrate performance data.

Over two thirds of respondents (68%) named optimising cost and cashflow as a leading priority. This was shortly followed by strengthening supplier or partner collaboration (55%) and improving environmental and social impact (53%).

Interestingly in contrast to the AQPC report earlier, this report respondents selected commercial and price performance (47%) as the highest ranked importance area for supplier performance, with only 16% focussing on quality and delivery (each).

Another interesting takeaway was that 37% analysed the supplier performance data monthly, with 28% doing so quarterly. One of the biggest improvement strategies  identified was to have regular feedback sessions with their suppliers (59%). I personally was surprised that the monthly review was only 37%, I expected that number to be higher, especially when the report also says 73% said that supplier performance data positively impacted business credibility and 72% said it improved supply resilience. Makes sense then that the top improvement area was to have more regular meetings!

Gartner Business Quarterly

The business quarterly is a great read for getting a handle on how business are reacting to the world around them. Goes into huge amounts of differing areas.

In this quarters “navigating a world without truth” it goes into detail about AI and trust in the technology, how to boost organisational culture, and what are organisations doing to cope with all the disruption.

For this question, basically they are adapting their strategies/ 79% are planning revamped strategies by the end of 2024. Therefore procurement leaders will need to be across these changes, to see how their category strategies will need to pivot to adapt.

AI is a big area (wait what…!) and this report covers it in a number of ways. From identifying where their survey thinks headcount reductions will be (Customer service, finance and product/service ops) to the fact that 84% are engaged in or planning AI initiatives over the next 3 years and to a poll that returned 42% said the biggest AI concern was data privacy.

There was a section on organisational culture. As someone who spent a bit of time studying Charles Handy and understanding organisations for various qualifications, organisational dynamics and culture is an interesting area. Some stats jumped out from this section.

  • Eighty-two percent of board members agree organizational culture is very or extremely important.
  • Only 53% of employees trust their organizations
  • Only 63% of organizations trust their employees

Meaning their may well be mutual mis trust. Trust is critical for buyer seller dynamics as it is for employer/employee and there are some interesting case studies from PayPal, DSM, and Laurentian Bank as to how their organisations seek to build trust and improve culture.

Hackett Digital World Class

This annual study into benchmarking digital world class organisations against their peers, is always well received especially when so many research pieces are call out the digitalisation drive that many organisations are prioritising now.

Digital World Class® procurement organizations run at a 21% lower cost than the peer group, with 32% fewer full-time equivalent (FTE) staff. Yet, they deliver much more: greater spend influence, faster time to value and more focus on value creation. It’s no surprise that they are 1.7 times more likely to be considered a valued business partner. These organizations returned two times more overall cost savings and increased the savings contribution from 2023 to 2024 at a rate that is 4.5 times faster than the peer group.

Digital World Class procurement organizations invest 15% of their total operating budget in technology. A closer look at the composition of procurement operating cost as a percentage of spend highlights a 32% greater technology cost for top-performing procurement organizations as compared to the peer group.

Some other numbers that jumped out to me. Digital world class organisations:

  • Had 21% more spend influenced
  • 15% more likely to involved earlier
  • 59% less savings lost due to maverick spending

It’s a great read and one worth spending the time to go through and consider what it means

 

As always reach out to discuss more, and always happy to hear your thoughts!

entry.title: Real-Time Support for Procurement Webcast: Introduction to the SAP Community!
entry.content:
Real-Time Support for Procurement Webcast Series

SAP Support's Digital Strategy team successfully hosted a live webcast on Aug 22, 2024. During this live session, we covered:

  • Why and how you should use the SAP Community
  • How to get registered
  • Asking questions to find help
  • Finding and following relevant tags 
  • Commenting & Answering 
  • Blogs on the SAP Community
  • Gamification, direct messages, and settings 
  • Where to find resources to become an SAP Community super-user! 

Watch or rewatch the webcast here (17:45, English)!

Download the slides

Get registered today!

 

What is the SAP Community and why should you register?

SAP Community Tour

Benefits to joining with absolutely no cost:

  • Connect and engage with like-minded professionals
  • Ask questions and share your product knowledge
  • Participate in discussions with SAP experts
  • Follow topics that align with your interests and/or location
  • Learn and stay current on SAP products through blogs
  • Explore and join groups, events, and fun contests

As a user of SAP Ariba, SAP Business Network, and/or SAP Fieldglass products, check out the Spend Management content page. Here, you can Search, look through the different boards for Q&A or Blogs, see recent feed, find learning material, and more. 

 

Why use the SAP Community for Procurement products?

There are a lot of different Support options and tools available to you, no matter how you use SAP products. Find how-to information, FAQs, Documentation, tutorials, and guided help in our Help Centers for Ariba Suppliers/ Buyers or Fieldglass users. 

Getting Support from SAP is easy and useful, but there are times when you want advice or guidance from fellow users. Maybe your site configuration is unique and you want to see how other companies handle a certain site setting, or you are looking for best practices about enabling a new feature, or you're an integrated customer looking for help on multiple SAP solutions. 

The SAP Community is completely free to use and offers you a vast network of users and experts! 

 

Your turn!

Subscribe to tags to customize your experience. 

Click on one of the tags from this blog directly to start following content, or Subscribe manually: 

  1. Visit the SAP Community.
  2. Click Products and Technology > All SAP Managed Tags.
  3. Find and click on a tag you're interested in.
  4. Click Subscribe. 

Look for all our tags starting with SAP Ariba, SAP Business Network, and SAP Fieldglass! 

Ask questions for help, get advice on best practices, and consult with other SAP users and experts. 

A well-written question includes:

  • Clear & descriptive title
  • Steps taken that led to your question
  • Screenshots & specific error messages 
  • Specific details (product version, customizations, roles or user authorizations, etc)
  • Correct SAP managed tag(s) added
  • No personally identifiable information (vendor details, user contact information, account numbers, etc)

Share your product knowledge and showcase your expertise by answering questions and commenting. 

Follow the SAP Community Rules of Engagement whenever you are participating.

See all our how-to videos

Hope to see you engaging in the Spend Management area of SAP Community soon! 

Resources from the webcast: 

  • Access the on-demand presentation
  • SAP Community tour
  • How-to Community video tutorials
  • Rules of Engagement

 

Real-Time Support for Procurement Webcast Series

Check out our webcast calendar for upcoming presentations!
Did you miss a webcast? Review our archive for all past content!

entry.title: Maximizing M&A Success: How SAP Solutions Drive Growth and Effectiveness
entry.content:
Maximizing M&A Success:  How SAP Solutions Drive Growth and Effectiveness

Authored by:  Christina Grace, SAP Global Finance Value Advisor & Marie Trojani, SAP Global HCM Value Advisor

Mergers and acquisitions (M&A) are complex transactions that can significantly alter the trajectory of a business. While they present opportunities for growth, diversification, and enhanced market presence, they also come with inherent challenges that can hinder success. One critical factor in navigating these challenges is the effective integration of systems, processes, people, and cultures. SAP offers a robust framework that can significantly enhance a company’s effectiveness for companies pursuing an M&A strategy. This blog explores how SAP facilitates successful M&A outcomes in the areas of Finance and HR and discusses potential inflection points in the journey to ensure success.

Understanding the M&A Landscape

Before delving into how SAP can assist during the M&A process, it's important to understand the typical M&A landscape. The process generally involves several phases:

  1. Planning and Strategy Development: Identifying potential targets and assessing strategic fit. This includes an assessment of current systems, people, and processes in both organizations. The goal should be to identify gaps and overlaps that could impact integration.  This phase also involves engaging key stakeholders from both organizations to ensure buy-in and prepare teams for the upcoming changes.
  2. Due Diligence: Analyzing financials, operations, and risks associated with the target company.  This analysis should provide insights into the target company’s strengths and weaknesses.  During this phase, data management tools can be used to perform scenario modeling of the two companies, set KPI goals, measure compliance, and ensure data harmonization.  A risk assessment should be performed to evaluate potential risks associated with the acquisition. Identifying risks early allows for proactive management strategies. That’s also at this stage that you can evaluate the cultural fit between the two companies.
  3. Integration and Strategy Management: Merging systems, processes, people, and cultures post-acquisition. Develop a comprehensive integration roadmap that outlines timelines, responsibilities, and key milestones. This roadmap should leverage GenAI integration capabilities to ensure a seamless transition. Prepare a training and support plan to educate employees about new systems and processes. Effective training will minimize disruptions and foster a sense of ownership among staff.  Real-time monitoring of the integration process is essential and allows for quick adjustments if any issues arise.  Establish feedback mechanisms to gather input from employees about the integration process. Utilize these insights to refine strategies and address concerns promptly. Particularly, HR concerns can appear quickly around job redundancies, salary gaps, talent retention for critical roles and employee morale.
  4. Post-Merger Evaluation: Assessing the success of the integration and overall M&A performance. After integration, evaluate the performance of the combined entity using advanced analytics tools. Assess whether M&A goals have been met and identify areas for further improvement, realizing greater synergies, and operating more efficiently.  Perform a cultural integration review by assessing employee engagement and satisfaction.  This allows companies to gauge the success of cultural integration, to gather insights, and to address any lingering issues.

Each phase presents unique challenges that SAP addresses with agility, transparency, and scalability.

The Role of SAP in M&A

SAP provides a comprehensive suite of integrated solutions designed to enhance operational efficiency, drive revenue growth, harmonize data, and improve real-time insights and overall business intelligence. Here’s how SAP Finance and HR solutions can specifically help companies pursuing an M&A strategy:

Seamless Integration of Systems

Challenge: One of the biggest hurdles in M&A is integrating disparate IT systems from merging organizations. Incompatible systems can lead to inefficiencies, data silos, and disruptions in business operations.

SAP Solution: SAP’s integrated platform enables organizations to unify disparate systems under one umbrella. With tools such as SAP S/4HANA, businesses can achieve real-time data integration and consistency, allowing for streamlined operations across the newly formed entity.  In the HR community, harmonizing different payroll, benefits, and performance management systems requires strategic planning and meticulous execution to avoid disruptions and maintain employee satisfaction.  This seamless integration reduces downtime and ensures continuity of service, which is critical during transitional phases.

Efficient Data Management

Challenge: During an M&A integration, organizations face an overwhelming amount of data that needs to be migrated, analyzed, and integrated. Poor data management can result in costly mistakes and compliance issues.

SAP Solution: SAP’s data management tools, including SAP Business Technology Platform, provide robust capabilities for data migration, cleansing, and integration. These solutions ensure that data from both companies are harmonized and accessible, allowing decision-makers to derive insights quickly and effectively. Real-time analytics further enable businesses to identify trends and make informed decisions swiftly.

Streamlined Financial Reporting

Challenge: Accurate financial reporting is essential during M&A, both for compliance and for assessing the financial health of the combined entity. However, multiple financial systems and reporting standards can complicate this process.

SAP Solution: SAP solutions offer integrated financial management tools that facilitate streamlined reporting and compliance with international financial standards. With SAP Analytics Cloud, businesses can create real-time financial dashboards, enabling stakeholders to monitor KPIs and other financial metrics seamlessly. This capability is crucial for transparent communication with investors, internal stakeholders, and regulatory bodies.

Enhanced Operational Efficiency

Challenge: Post-M&A, organizations often encounter operational redundancies and inefficiencies due to overlapping processes and systems.  Furthermore, redundancies and restructuring can lead to workforce reductions, which may result in the loss of valuable talent and institutional knowledge.

SAP Solution: SAP’s process automation capabilities can help identify and eliminate redundancies. For example, with SAP Ariba, companies can enhance procurement processes, while SAP SuccessFactors can streamline human resources management. HR must manage these transitions delicately, ensuring fair and compliant processes while maintaining operational continuity. These solutions foster operational efficiencies that lead to cost savings and improved productivity across the organization. 

Change Management Support

Challenge: M&A transactions often lead to significant challenges to company culture, processes, and personnel. Concerns about the unknown and resistance to change can derail integration efforts. 

SAP Solution:  Fostering employee engagement and loyalty can be key to overcoming these obstacles. Initiatives to support change management, such as training programs and team-building activities, are essential but must be carefully tailored to address the specific needs and concerns of the workforce.  SAP offers tools to support change management, including SAP Enable Now, which provides training and support resources for employees.  This tool allows organizations to create custom training materials and resources that can facilitate a smoother transition. By preparing employees for the changes ahead, companies can mitigate resistance and foster a more collaborative environment.

Risk Management and Compliance

Challenge: M&A transactions are inherently risky, and the complexities of integrating different compliance standards can lead to potential legal and regulatory issues.

SAP Solution: SAP’s risk management tools, such as SAP GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance), enable businesses to assess and manage risks effectively. These solutions provide a framework for identifying compliance gaps and ensuring adherence to regulatory requirements, thereby protecting the organization from potential liabilities.

Cultural Integration

Challenge: Merging different company cultures can be one of the most challenging aspects of M&A. Misalignment can lead to employee dissatisfaction and turnover.  M&A often herald significant organizational transformation, presenting numerous challenges for HR and employees. Merging distinct corporate cultures can lead to misalignment, confusion, and conflicts. HR must navigate the complex task of creating a cohesive environment where employees from both organizations feel valued and understood.

SAP Solution: While SAP solutions primarily focus on operational aspects, they can also support cultural integration indirectly. Tools like SAP SuccessFactors provide insights into employee engagement and performance, enabling leadership to address cultural challenges proactively. By fostering open communication and collaboration through these platforms, organizations can work towards aligning their cultures more effectively.

Job Security

Challenge: Another critical issue is job security. Employees often face anxiety and uncertainty about their future roles, leading to decreased morale and productivity. There is a higher risk for top talent to leave the company during times of uncertainty.

SAP Solution: Transparent communication from HR is vital to mitigate these concerns, as well as a comprehensive view into employee data such as competencies and job profiles. Analytics and AI can facilitate informed decisions and proactively alert if there are potential risks. Yet finding the balance between transparency and confidentiality can be challenging.

In navigating all of these challenges, Finance, HR and IT all play a crucial role in facilitating a smooth transition, fostering a positive organizational culture, and maintaining employee well-being and productivity during M&A activities.

Benefits of SAP Solutions in M&A

  • Efficiency: Streamlines operations and reduces integration time
  • Cost Savings: Minimizes redundancies and operational costs
  • Real-Time Insights: Provides data-driven decision-making capabilities
  • Risk Mitigation: Enhances compliance and governance
  • Employee Engagement: Supports smooth cultural integration through training and engagement tools

Conclusion

In today’s fast-paced business environment, the success of M&A hinges on effective integration and management of systems, people, data, and processes. In an era where adaptability and efficiency are paramount, embracing SAP Finance and HR solutions not only supports immediate M&A goals but also lays a solid foundation for sustained success in an ever-evolving marketplace.

SAP offers a comprehensive toolkit for companies navigating the multifaceted complexities and challenges of M&A, enabling organizations to streamline operations, enhance decision-making, and drive overall success.  By leveraging SAP’s integrated tools, companies can navigate the complexities of M&A with greater confidence and effectiveness. By facilitating integration, enhancing data management, and supporting operational efficiency, SAP helps organizations achieve their M&A objectives while laying the groundwork for long-term success.  As organizations continue to pursue growth through acquisitions, those that adopt SAP solutions will be well-positioned to realize the full potential of their M&A strategies, fostering a more agile and competitive future.

entry.title: SAP Ariba 2411 Release Key Innovations Preview
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba community!

We're thrilled to announce some fantastic new features and enhancements for Q4 2024 across various SAP Ariba modules. These updates are designed to streamline your procurement processes, enhance supplier management, and provide deeper insights into supplier relationships. Let's take a high-level look at the most impactful innovations in this release.

For those of you wanting to explore specific functionalities in detail, we have provided links to dedicated blogs that dive deeper into each area. Let's take a look!

SAP Spend Control Tower

We're excited to share the latest development with you SAP Spend Control Tower. This new tool is designed to merge all your payment and supplier data into a single source of truth, enriched with AI-enabled spend classification and supplier insights. With intuitive dashboards and advanced machine learning models, it’s easier than ever to streamline our processes, cut procurement costs, manage supplier risks, and keep an eye on our diversity and sustainability goals.

Having a unified data source might just be the key to overcoming some of our biggest challenges. SAP Spend Control Tower takes the powerful analytics and reporting capabilities of the old SAP Ariba Spend Analysis solution and pushes them even further. If you're curious to learn more about how it could help us transform our spend management, I'd recommend checking out the full details on our SAP Spend Control Tower product page.

Spend overview dashboard in SAP Spend Control Tower

SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance and Supplier Risk

Intelligent Recommendations Based on Supplier Risk Score

SAP Ariba Supplier Risk now offers intelligent recommendations for suppliers experiencing significant changes in risk scores. These new insights help buyers proactively identify and mitigate potential risks.

New recommendations section in the SAP Ariba Supplier Risk dashboard

SAP Business Network Certificate Information in the Supplier Profile Summary

The supplier profile summary now features added visibility into certificates maintained in SAP Business Network profiles. The new “Business Network Certificates” panel provides additional data points for supplier assessments, offering a comprehensive view directly from their SAP Business Network profile.

Business network certificates in the supplier profile summary

See the SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance & Supplier Risk 2411 Release: Key Feature Highlights blog for more information on these and other supplier management features

SAP Ariba Source to Contract

Enhancements to Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) in SAP Ariba Category Management

We're excited to introduce enhancements to our Generative AI capabilities in SAP Ariba Category Management. You can now selectively generate articles for tools like Category Segmentation, Market Dynamics, and Cost Structure on demand, improving performance and reducing costs associated with producing AI-generated content.

Enhancements to Generative Artificial Intelligence in SAP Ariba Category Management

Ability to Create Category Strategy Details from the Develop Strategy and Plan App in SAP Ariba Category Management

Our new Category Strategy Details tool in the Develop Strategy and Plan app empowers category managers to create and manage multiple category-specific strategy details aligned with the overall business strategy.

Category strategy details in SAP Ariba Category Management

Ability to View, Analyze, and Manage Initiatives Across Categories in SAP Ariba Category Management

Manage your initiatives through the new Initiatives Overview app, providing comprehensive tools for tracking, optimizing, and visualizing data.

Initiatives overview in SAP Ariba Category Management

 

Create Optimization Scenarios for Multi-Round Bidding Events in Guided Sourcing

Generate optimization scenarios for multi-round bidding events, including editing constraints and updating scenarios with the latest data.

Enhancements to Grading and Scoring in Guided Sourcing

Improved grading features for guided sourcing, including offline grading for primary bids and rolled-up section level grading for alternate bids.

See the SAP Ariba Source to Contract 2411 Release Key Innovations Preview blog for more information on these and other source to contract features

SAP Ariba Procure to Order

Support for PunchOut Items in SAP Ariba Shopping Mobile App

You can now purchase items from external supplier catalogs through Level 1 PunchOut catalogs directly from the SAP Ariba Shopping mobile app. This enhancement broadens your product range for mobile shopping while ensuring compliance and approvals are managed through the normal Ariba checkout process.

View and Edit Requisition Line Items in SAP Ariba Shopping Mobile App

The SAP Ariba Shopping mobile app now provides detailed line item information in the requisition details screen and allows users to edit line items directly from the checkout screen. This update enhances flexibility and transparency in the mobile requisitioning process.

See the SAP Ariba Procure to Order 2411 Release Key Innovations blog for more information on these and other features

For further reading and resources, check out the links below and dive deeper into each specific innovation.

Resources:
    • Register for the Early Release Series for 2411 release
    • Resources:
      • Feature related materials
        • SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)
        • Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)
